This video explains three ways to buy a new car in Australia: * Buying Outright: Paying with cash. It's the cheapest long-term option as you avoid interest, but requires a large upfront payment. * Car Loan: Borrowing money to buy. You own the car immediately but pay extra in interest, making it the most expensive option long-term. * Novated Lease: A salary packaging arrangement where car payments and running costs are deducted from your pre-tax income. This offers tax savings, especially for higher earners, but you don't own the car unless you pay a final "residual" payment. Recommendation: * Buy outright if you have the cash and plan to keep the car long-term. * A novated lease can be smart if you're a high-income earner and like upgrading your car regularly. * Car loans are generally not recommended as you're borrowing for an asset that loses value.

You can't talk about staying invested because of the 10 best days of the market without also talking about avoiding the 10 worst days. In fact, most of the best days in the market took place during periods of market downturns and if you weren't invested and missed that best day, you actually would still net positive if you weren't invested in the market during the entire bear market cycle. All of the wealthiest people of the world rebalance their portfolios during periods of market volatility and hold less stocks and more cash. They don't follow the "stay invested" rule, and so neither should you. It is a good idea to adjust your portfolio when market conditions look bad. Just don't do it because of the hype, do it because you are doing the research and understand when the market is too expensive vs cheap. Funnily enough, missing the 10 worst days in the market has a more significant positive impact on your portfolio than missing the 10 best days has as a negative impact. That kind of suggests that you are actually better off pulling back on investments during bear markets and that it will have a higher expected value on your portfolio.

Stocks, as they are more scalable than real estate. You should explain your claimed tax "saving" example is a timing saving. All other things being equal all you are doing is changing the timing of capital gains tax payable i.e. deferring by choosing highest cost base shares as being sold and holding on to lower cost base shares to sell at the higher capital gain later on. Timing is potentially important depending on the seller's circumstances i.e. might suit then to defer or bring forward taxable income etc. And there's the time value of money to consider as well (which supports reducing capital gains now). However, the point stands you should be highlighting you are fine tuning the timing of paying tax rather than it being a permanent saving.

Cautionary note: The use of mortgage redraw funding to invest in equities raises your personal balance sheet leverage ratios. 1) If you lose your job (or get so ill you can't work) and can't maintain the mortgage payments, you will might need to deleverage or even sell your home. Needing to take this drastic step at a bad time in the share market or in the housing market can lock in capital losses. 2) With a higher outstanding balance on your home mortgage, your mortgage payments will be higher. 3) If mortgage rates climb (not fall as hoped) the servicing of the higher outstanding mortgage will further reduce your disposable income even further. Consider all these factors before using leverage to buy volatile assets (equities, corporate bonds, etc).

One thing that you failed to mention is that almost all ASX company franking credits are franked at 30%, the company tax rate for large companies. So generally if your income is under $45,000 per year you will get a franking credit refund. If your marginal tax rate is at 30% then no refund and if your marginal tax rate is higher than 30% you will pay tax, because while the franking credit is a tax credit that amount is also added to your taxable income, thereby attracting tax at your marginal rate.

Real estate as investment is static because of physical location. If the prices of real estate go up, the rest of the real estate area goes up similarly. Investing in stock is not the same. Some companies go up some go down and some are just static for months and years. Unless the real estate produces money actively (e.g. rental) than it is mostly a storage mechanism. Buy a house now at 1M, if it goes to 2M in 3 years, the rest of the similar house in similar areas are going to the same value. So to realize the growth you have to exit that location and go spend the money somewhere else. Not the same with stock.

I dont want to blow this out of proportion - but MOAT isn't a dividend ETF, and the fact it has a 5.8% div yield is NOT stable. MOAT is a defensive/undervalued stock ETF - it basically buys and sells companies that are both safe/defensible and undervalued - it tracks a morningstar index for this. So the dividends come from 3 places 1- Some companies DO pay dividends, but these tend to not be high yield companies (unlike VHY) 2- Capital gains from redistribution (buying/selling) 3- Return on capital So yes, while MOAT has had around an $8 dividend for the last 3 years - before that it was anywhere between $1 and 40c - so yes it has dividends, but these arent guaranteed, its been a benefit of holding the ETF - not a promise going forward.
